title: Learning by Owning in a Lemons Market
abstract: We study market dynamics when an owner learns about the quality of her asset over time. Since this information is private, the owner sells strategically to a less informed buyer following sufficient negative information. In response, market prices feature a ‚ÄúU-shape‚ÄĚ and trading probabilities a ‚Äúhump-shape‚ÄĚ with respect to the time to sale. As the owner initially acquires greater information, buyers suffer greater adverse selection, and prices fall accordingly. Eventually, the probability of an informed sale shrinks, and prices rebound. We provide evidence consistent with our model in markets for residential real estate, venture capital investments, and construction equipment.

article.introduction:
In many settings, asset ownerscollect private information about the quality of their assets. For example, a homeowner may learn about the desirability of her neighborhood by exploring the local community and interacting with her neighbors. Likewise, a venture capital (VC) investor may learn about the viability of a portfolio company by maintaining a close eye on company developments and company personnel. Finally, a firm may uncover new information about the quality or productivity of a capital asset such as a machine or vehicle by operating it over time.1In each of these contexts, the owner of an asset has access to a stream of private information that gradually increases the degree of information asymmetry between the owner and prospective¬†buyers.
Focusing on this setting with owner learning, we show that asset prices follow a U-shape and trading volumes a hump-shape with respect to the length of time an asset is owned prior to sale. Initially, prices decline and trading activity increases as owners sell their assets strategically in response to new negative information. However, after enough time has passed, buyers infer that assets that remain unsold are likely to be of higher quality, and hence, prices subsequently rebound as trading activity¬†declines.
Suppose that an asset owner possesses a single indivisible asset of unknown quality and that, over time, the owner acquires new information about the asset's value. In response to this information, the owner continually decides either to keep the asset‚ÄĒwhich entails continued learning and the option to sell at future prices‚ÄĒor to sell the asset strategically to a less informed buyer. Since buyers do not directly observe the owner's motivation for selling, the market price of the asset at each point in time reflects buyers' beliefs regarding the quality of the asset conditional on both the owner's decision to sell and the length of time that the owner possessed the asset prior to selling. By conditioning on the length of ownership, buyers adjust their beliefs in response to both the owner's increasing private information and the owner's dynamic selling¬†strategy.
Now consider the price path implied by the owner's optimal selling decision. When the owner first acquires the asset, her informational advantage relative to prospective buyers is small due to her limited involvement with the asset. Since the owner's private information is limited, the market price closely reflects the asset's ex ante expected value. Information asymmetry then increases in response to the owner's private learning, leading the price to fall over time as buyers become increasingly concerned that the owner may be selling due to learned negative information. Since the owner sells strategically in response to negative information, buyers interpret the owner's decisionnotto sell at a point in time as good information about the value of the asset. After enough time passes, the good news implied by a longer holding period dominates the increase in information asymmetry, and hence buyers' beliefs‚ÄĒand, in turn, prices‚ÄĒbegin to¬†rise.
In sum, with private learning, price dynamics are predictable and U-shaped, even in a setting in which agents are rational and anticipate future price changes. That price changes are predictable may seem counter intuitive: Why does the owner not sell sooner when prices are falling and not sell later when prices are rising? While price changes are predictable, dividend payments (be they housing or equipment services, investment payouts, or literal dividends) are not, because these payments depend on an asset's unknown quality. During the initial period of falling prices, even the owner of a poorly performing asset still holds a somewhat optimistic belief regarding the value of the asset's expected dividend flow. While the capital gain at this time is negative, the expected dividend yield is high, offsetting the future decline in the asset's price. As more time passes, the owner of a lower quality asset is likely to receive additional negative information, which further reduces her belief regarding the asset's quality. When beliefs have fallen far enough and the expected dividend yield is low enough, prices must rise so that a marginal owner earns the same expected cum dividend return whether selling initially when prices are falling or later when prices are rising. Importantly, even when prices are rising, prices rise less quickly than the owner's required rate of return so that delaying a sale is not strictly¬†optimal.
Consistent with the model, Figures1,2to3provide evidence of a U-shaped price path in the markets for residential real estate, VC investment, and heavy equipment used in construction and farming. Figure1, Panel A, displays the relationship between calendar-time-adjusted holding-period returns on single-family homes and the length of ownership at the time of sale. For single-family homes, cumulative holding-period returns are adjusted using purchase year-quarter fixed effects and sale year-quarter fixed effects to remove the impact of calendar time on holding-period returns.2Initially, adjusted holding-period returns for single-family homes fall steeply as the length of ownership increases from one day to three years before rebounding as the length of ownership increases from 3 to 15 years. Figure1, Panel B, displays the corresponding frequency of home sales over different holding periods. As in the model, the frequency of trade rises as holding-period returns decline and subsequently decreases as holding-period returns rebound so that the frequency of trade is largest approximately when returns are¬†lowest.
Figure2, Panel A, shows a similar relation using the premoney valuation of a venture-financed start-up and the length of ownership at the time of a change in the lead VC investor (which we interpret as a sale of the portfolio company from the lead VC during a previous round of funding to a new lead VC in a subsequent round).3For venture-financed start-ups, cumulative holding-period returns fall dramatically as the length of ownership increases from three months to one year and then rebound by roughly the same amount when the length of ownership increases from 1 to 2.5 years. As in the market for single-family homes, the frequency of VC sales (see Figure2, Panel B) rises and falls as the length of ownership increases, again consistent with the model-implied rise and fall in the probability of a strategic sale over time. Finally, Figure3, Panel A, plots the relation between age-adjusted holding-period returns on heavy equipment and the length of ownership at the time of sale.4For heavy equipment, cumulative holding-period returns are adjusted using purchase-age fixed effects and sale-age fixed effects to reduce the impact of physical depreciation in the quality of the asset over time on holding-period returns. As in the previous examples, adjusted holding-period returns feature a U-shape, falling gradually for holding periods up to four years before increasing as the length of ownership increases from four to 10 years.5While the frequencies of trade for single-family homes and venture-financed startups exhibit a hump-shape over time, the frequency of sales for heavy equipment (see Figure3, Panel B) is strictly decreasing in the length of ownership. In the context of the model, we show that the frequency of sales can be strictly declining rather than humped-shaped when owners sell too often for reasons unrelated to asset quality. The evidence therefore suggests that the fraction of trade resulting from information-based selling is smaller in the market for construction equipment than in either the market for residential real estate or the market for venture assets.6
While we provide new evidence of a U-shaped price path in the markets for residential real estate, VC investment, and heavy equipment, the model is also related to an established literature that studies prices and trading activity in durable goods markets. In particular, Emons and Sheldon (2009) find that the hazard function of new car ownership increases for two quarters of ownership and then declines over the next six quarters. Consistent with this evidence, our model predicts that the likelihood of sale is initially increasing as owners increase strategic selling in response to greater private information and subsequently decreasing as strategic selling subsides.7Moreover, Emons and Sheldon (2009) show that higher-quality cars are less likely to be resold quickly, while Genesove (1993) shows that automobiles resold multiple times have lower prices than comparable automobiles resold only once. While in our model the asset trades only once, the model predicts that a lower quality asset trades on average more quickly and at a lower price than a higher quality asset. Similarly, Porter and Sattler (1999) find that automobile models that depreciate more quickly also experience greater turnover. While this positive relationship between depreciation and trading volume is typically interpreted as evidence against adverse selection in used car markets, our model suggests that when owners learn slowly over time and sell strategically in response to their private information, prices fall furthest when owners sell most aggressively following new negative information.8,9
The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. SectionIdiscusses related literature. SectionIIintroduces the modeling environment and highlights the agents' strategies and belief processes. SectionIIIprovides our equilibrium analysis. In SectionIV, we discuss the key modeling assumptions and consider alternative payoff processes that imply different information structures. SectionVconcludes. AppendixAcontains important notation used in AppendicesBtoGto complete the proofs omitted from the main¬†text.
article.I. Literature:
Our model contributes to a growing literature that studies dynamic markets with adverse selection. In particular, Janssen and Roy (2002) show that when sellers are endowed with fixed private information and do not learn over time, equilibrium prices increase over time as owners of higher-type assets delay trade in order to signal their type. While all assets are eventually sold, trade is delayed, and the market equilibrium is therefore inefficient. Camargo and Lester (2014) find related market dynamics in a decentralized market where buyers and sellers are matched randomly each period. As in the centralized market of Janssen and Roy (2002), trade is delayed, and prices and average quality increase over time. The U-shaped price path that arises in our model with owner learning lies in contrast to the increasing price path in these models with fixed private information. While the eventual rise in prices mimics that in the existing literature, prices fall initially as the owner acquires greater private information and the lemons problem becomes more severe.10
Our model is also related to recent work that investigates learning and reputation in lemons markets. Notably, Daley and Green (2012,2016) consider a model in which public news about the quality of a seller's asset is released over time and construct an equilibrium that involves periods of complete market breakdown. Asriyan, Green, and Fuchs (2017) analyze public learning across markets when asset values are correlated and show that greater market transparency can lead to greater delays in trade. In contrast, we consider market dynamics when owners, rather than buyers, learn privately over time. Thus, ours is a setting in which information asymmetry is initially increasing before being resolved in the long run. Bar-Isaac (2003) provides a related model in which a monopolist decides to either remain in the market and sell a good of unknown quality or exit the market. When the monopolist is informed about her type, a low-quality seller plays a mixed selling strategy at a lower reputation threshold (as in Daley and Green (2012)), offsetting the flow of negative information.11If the seller is instead uninformed and learns at the same rate as buyers, the seller exits the market following a string of low-quality signals. Our model is similar in that the owner of a poorly performing asset sells strategically over time in response to continuing negative information even if the asset might eventually be revealed to have high¬†quality.
Among dynamic models, our model is most similar to theories by Plantin (2009) and Hwang (2018) who consider‚ÄĒas we do here‚ÄĒmarkets in which owners learn privately over time about the value of their assets. Plantin (2009) shows that private learning leads to an illiquidity discount for privately placed debt securities and that this discount is larger for more risky assets. Hwang (2018) shows that, when the quality of information is high enough, owner learning leads to a crash and recovery in prices and trading activity over time. While the crash in prices in Hwang (2018) is associated with a corresponding freeze in market liquidity and trading activity, the U-shape in our model stems from an increase in the likelihood of a strategic sale. Thus, our model suggests that trading activity and prices are inversely related (rather than positively related) when owners sell strategically to less informed buyers following new negative information about the quality of their assets. In contrast to existing theories, our model can therefore explain the U-shape in log holding-period returns and hump-shape in frequencies of trade presented in Figures1and2.
Finally, many of the models discussed above (including Hwang (2018)) begin with an assumption of common knowledge of gains from trade (as is common in models studying adverse selection; see, for example, Akerlof (1970)). In a dynamic context, this assumption requires that buyers observe (and condition on) the time at which these gains from trade begin, perhaps through the realization of a publicly observable shock such as a bankruptcy filing or following an observable decline in sectoral productivity. In contrast, in our model, buyers need only observe the time at which the owner takes possession of her asset to formulate their beliefs regarding the quality of the owner's asset at the time of sale. As such, our model applies well to the markets for VC investment, residential real estate, and heavy equipment‚ÄĒmarkets for which the dates of funding rounds or real estate/equipment transactions are readily available but observations of owner-specific liquidity needs are unlikely to be observed. In this context, our model also relates to the informed trading models of Glosten and Milgrom (1985) and Back and Baruch (2004) in that trade arises from both agents with private information and agents selling for reasons unrelated to asset quality. In our model, however, the arrival rate of an informed sale is determined strategically in response to the evolution of the owner's private¬†information.
article.II. The Model:
Time is infinite, continuous, and indexed byt$t$. The economy consists of a single asset owner and a competitive market of prospective buyers. All agents are risk-neutral and discount future cash flows at rater>0$r>0$. At timet=0$t=0$, the owner is endowed with a single indivisible asset (e.g., a home, venture investment, or vehicle). The asset's type is given byőł‚ąą{H,L}$\theta \in \lbrace H,L\rbrace$. We refer to a type-H$H$asset as ‚Äúhigh quality‚ÄĚ and to a type-L$L$asset as ‚Äúlow quality.‚ÄĚ The common prior probability that the asset is high quality isQ‚ąą(0,1)$Q\in (0,1)$. A high-quality asset generates lump-sum payoffs (e.g., housing or equipment services, investment payoffs, or dividends)Y>0$Y>0$that arrive according to a Poisson process with rateőĽ>0$\lambda >0$, while a low-quality asset never produces a¬†payoff.
We assume that the owner learns privately about the value of her asset through the arrival (or nonarrival) of a payoff breakthrough because this allows for a tractable characterization of equilibrium trading behavior. In reality, owners are likely to learn about the quality of their assets through a variety of good and bad news arrivals, with the precise structure of payoffs and information depending on the asset itself. For this reason, in SectionIV, we discuss (and in theInternet Appendix, we formally analyze) alternative payoff processes that imply different information structures and argue that the breakthrough model is special only in that bad news arrives slowly over time.12That is, if the information structure is such that the owner never learns too much negative information at one time, then the predictions for market prices and trading dynamics in such a setting will be the same as those derived here in a model with payoff¬†breakthroughs.
The owner in this setting is driven to sell her asset for one of two¬†reasons.
(i) Liquidity sale: At an exogenous Poisson intensityŌÄ>0$\pi >0$, the owner experiences a ‚Äúliquidity‚ÄĚ event at which time she is forced to sell immediately. The shock can be interpreted as a sudden need for liquidity on the part of the owner (perhaps due to the end of a limited partnership in the case of a VC investor) and/or as a discontinuous change in the owner's personal value for the asset (such as at the end of a construction project for the owner of heavy equipment).13Within the model, a liquidity sale has two defining features. First, it occurs for reasons unrelated to the asset's underlying type. Second, the shock's arrival is the private information of the owner and is unverifiable to prospective buyers. The latter feature provides a key departure from existing models studying dynamic trading under asymmetric information that assume common knowledge of gains from trade (see, for example, Fuchs and Skrzypacz (2015), Fuchs, Green, and Papanikolaou (2016), and Hwang (2018)).
(ii) Strategic sale: At each point in time, the owner may sell her asset ‚Äústrategically‚ÄĚ to a less informed buyer if the market price exceeds her value of continued ownership. For example, a venture capitalist may choose to sell her investment position in a start-up company if she learns that the company has failed to make adequate progress in its development of a new product or technology, since this information is less likely to be available to other outside¬†investors.
We now clarify the order of events within each infinitesimal period[t,t+dt)$[t,t+dt)$. First, the owner learns the realization of the liquidity shock and chooses whether to list her asset for sale. Recall that she must list her asset for sale following the liquidity shock and may also choose to list her asset for sale strategically if she believes that it is profitable to do so. LetX‚Č•0$X\ge 0$denote the stochastic time at which the owner lists her asset for sale, and lető©$\Omega$denote its cumulative distribution function (CDF). Importantly, the decision to list the asset for sale is irreversible. Once the owner chooses to sell her asset, she cannot reconsider her sales decision after observing buyers' bids. If the owner choosesnotto list her asset at timet$t$, then the asset's payoff is either realized or not, the owner updates her belief accordingly, and the game continues to the next infinitesimal period. If instead the ownerdoeslist her asset for sale, then after observing the owner's listing decision, buyers bid for the asset in an ascending auction, and the asset is awarded to a buyer with the highest bid.14
As is standard in much of the related literature, we assume that the winning buyer cannot resell the asset in the future, and as a result, the game ends after the asset is sold. In addition, we assume that trade incurs a transaction costc>0$c>0$paid by the winning buyer. This assumption implies that strategic selling is always inefficient. If all information were public, the owner would retain the asset up until the realization of the liquidity shock at which point she would list the asset for sale. However, information in this setting is not public, since the owner privately observes the asset's payoffs, and as a result, the owner may sell inefficiently early if her ownership value falls below the price buyers are willing to pay.15
In determining the optimal time to list her asset for sale, the owner takes as given the winning bid price (where we assume that bids are finite valued) that would arise at each point in time if she decided to sell in the future. We refer to this price as the ‚Äúmarket‚ÄĚ price and denote its value byp(t)$p(t)$. Taking the market price as given, the owner faces an optimal stopping¬†problem.
Since a low-quality asset never produces a payoffY$Y$, both the owner's belief regarding the quality of her asset and her optimal stopping rule depend only on whether she previously realized a payoff arrival. For this reason, in what follows we decompose the owner's problem into two meaningful subcases: one in which, at some times$s$, the owner first realizes the payoff breakthrough, and another in which the owner fails to realize the payoff breakthrough at any time. The reason for this division is that, following a payoff arrival, the owner learns with certainty that her asset is high quality, and hence, her belief is fixed at all future times. In addition, in the case that the owner fails to realize the payoff breakthrough, her belief falls deterministically over time as seen in equation¬†(2).16

Note that this restriction does not rule out mass points in the owner's strategiesőďL(t)$\Gamma _L(t)$andőďH(t)$\Gamma _H(t)$. Lemma 3. (Low-type strategy)The owner's strategy absent the payoff breakthrough may be a pure strategy or a mixed strategy depending on the chosen equilibrium.



Lemma3shows that the owner's strategy if she is low-type depends on the precise time at which she would have sold if she were high-type, since this dictates the selling times that are on or off the equilibrium path. article.IV. Discussion:
A key assumption of our model is the Poisson arrival of a dividend payoffY$Y$that perfectly and privately informs the owner that her asset is high quality. This raises two potential questions. First, what are some (approximate) real-world analogs of the Poisson dividends that are experienced by owners of high-quality assets? And second, is the Poisson payoff structure special, or should we expect similar market dynamics when dividends and information arrive under alternative payoff processes? While the answers to these two questions are closely related, we respond to each, in turn, in the following¬†sections.
First, as noted in SectionII, we assume that payoffs arrive via a type-specific Poisson dividend process simply because this payoff process allows for a tractable characterization of equilibrium asset prices and trading probabilities. While in reality, owners are likely to learn about the quality of their assets through a variety of good and bad news arrivals with the precise structure of payoffs and information depending on the asset itself, it is possible to take the model seriously and consider real-world analogs of the Poisson dividends as modeled. Taken literally, each Poisson arrival provides for the asset's owner a payoffY$Y$and information about the asset's type. In particular, because only a high-quality asset ever produces a payoff, a payoff perfectly informs the owner that her asset is of high quality. Mapping these dividends to a real-world setting is therefore most straightforward using our leading example of a startup company owned by a VC investor. In this case, a dividend payoff could be interpreted as a successful launch of a new product, a discovery of a scarce resource, or a development of a new drug compound, each of which could be quickly monetized by the firm's owners and each of which provides information about the firm's future profitability. For these examples to match precisely the model assumptions, it must be the case that (i) only a high-quality firm produces successful products, discovers valuable resources, or develops new drugs and (ii) the success or failure of these endeavors is observable only by the firms' owners (who do not need to publicly disclose their cash flows).
In the context of our other examples, housing and construction equipment, it is possible to view the payoffY$Y$in terms of the units of utility (such as housing or equipment services) that flow to the owner of the asset. For example, a high-quality tractor might be one that is strong enough to pull a plow through rough terrain or dependable enough to work year after year. In this case, the Poisson arrival could proxy for each time the owner of the tractor successfully plows her field. Likewise, a homeowner might get additional utility each time her neighbors organize a block party, decorate for the holidays, or stop by for a visit. A high-quality house then is one that is located in a highly social neighborhood, and the payoffY$Y$represents each instance of a party or¬†visit.
While the examples above match the Poisson dividend process to varying degrees, it is likely that owners of homes and equipment experience utility in a variety of ways. For example, a homeowner may experience a stream of utility from living in her home that is higher when visiting with her neighbors and lower when realizing that her roof leaks during a heavy storm. Similarly, an equipment owner may experience high utility when her tractor effortlessly traverses rough terrain and diminished utility each time her tractor fails to start. These examples are notable because they result in a change in the current utility of the asset's owner and also provide information about the asset's type. A roof that first leaks during a heavy spring storm may leak again if future storms produce similar rainfall. Likewise, a tractor that fails to start on a cold winter day may also fail to start on future cold days. While these examples therefore illustrate ways in which an owner learns privately over time from observing her asset's payoffs, they do not necessarily fit a model in which payoffs accrue only through perfectly informative Poisson dividends. In the following section, we argue that the breakthrough structure is special only in that negative information arrives gradually over time. That is, if the payment stream or utility flow produced by an asset is such that the owner never learns too much negative information all at once, then the qualitative implications of the model remain: prices are U-shaped over time and the probability of a strategic sale is¬†hump-shaped.
As discussed in the previous section, the payoffs (equivalently, utility flows) produced by housing and equipment assets may not be particularly amenable to our setting with Poisson dividends and perfectly revealing breakthroughs. For this reason, we investigate three alternative payoff processes that result in different information structures. In the interest of brevity, we relegate the complete analysis of these alternative models to theInternet Appendixand simply summarize their results¬†below.
First, we consider a setting in which both a high-qualityanda low-quality assets produce the dividend payment but at different rates,őĽH>őĽL>0$\lambda _H>\lambda _L>0$. In this case, it is no longer important that a low-quality start-up never produces a successful product, discovers a valuable resource, or develops a new drug. All that matters is that a low-quality start-up produces such an outcome less often than does a high-quality start-up. From the owner's perspective, each dividend payment or successful outcome therefore increases her belief that the firm is high quality, while each instance without an arrival decreases her belief. While we are unable to analytically characterize an equilibrium in this setting, in theInternet Appendix, we show through numerical simulation that, with theseimperfectlyinformative Poisson arrivals, the market price features a U-shape and the probability of trade a hump-shape over the owner's length of ownership, just as in the base model with perfectly informative¬†breakthroughs.
The reason we obtain similar qualitative results in both the preceding models and the base model is that in each case, the owner learns negative information gradually over time. While the owner's belief jumps up following a dividend payment in the models with Poisson arrivals, the owner's belief falls continuously each instant in which a dividend payment does not arrive. Positive information arrives in large, discrete ‚Äúlumps,‚ÄĚ but negative information arrives slowly. In the model in which payoffs follow a Brownian diffusion process, both positive and negative information arrive continuously over time, since the owner's belief in this case also follows a diffusion process. When the owner learns negative information gradually, it takes some time for the owner to receive enough negative information to be convinced that her asset is a lemon. This means that strategic selling begins slowly and gradually intensifies as the owner acquires more and more negative information, which leads to an initial decline in the market price. Eventually, the likelihood of a strategic sale shrinks, and the market price subsequently rebounds. These alternative models therefore provide some assurance that the market dynamics highlighted in the breakthrough model generalize more broadly to other payoff processes, which may be more applicable to certain assets such as houses and¬†equipment.
Finally, for completeness, we analyze one additional information structure‚ÄĒnamely, ‚Äúcostly breakdowns‚ÄĚ‚ÄĒwhich yields different market dynamics than either the models with Poisson arrivals or the model with Brownian learning. In this case, the asset produces a constant flow payoffydt$ydt$regardless of its quality. In addition, a low-quality asset imposes a cost on its owner ofK>0$K>0$with Poisson intensityőĽ>0$\lambda >0$while a high-quality asset never imposes this cost. Since only a low-quality asset produces an arrival, the first instance of the costly breakdown perfectly informs the owner that her asset is low quality. When negative information arrivesall at oncethrough the realization of a perfectly informative breakdown, the owner's belief jumps down in response to an arrival rather than falling gradually as in the breakthrough or Brownian models. This eliminates the initial gradual increase in the probability of a strategic sale, and as a result eliminates the initial decline in the market¬†price.
Thus, when negative information is too lumpy, private learning implies a strictly increasing path of prices over the owner's length of ownership rather than the U-shape derived in the breakthrough model and in the model with learning from a Brownian diffusion process. The pronounced U-shape in holding-period returns for single-family homes, VC investments, and heavy equipment therefore suggests that negative information in these markets arrives gradually over time, perhaps through the absence of a payoff breakthrough or in response to the continuous arrival of symmetric, incremental information (as in the model with Brownian learning).
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper investigates market prices and trading behavior when asset owners learn over time about the value of their assets. Since information is revealed privately, owners sell strategically to less informed buyers following new negative information. In response, asset prices and trading activity evolve predictably as a function of an owner's length of ownership prior to sale. When negative information is revealed gradually over time, prices initially decline as the owner begins selling strategically in response to sustained negative information and then rebound as the degree of asymmetric information in the market subsides. In contrast to previous models studying adverse selection in dynamic markets, asset prices and trading volumes are therefore negatively correlated in this setting, since greater strategic selling pushes market prices further downward. Consistent with these predictions, cumulative holding-period returns in the markets for residential real estate, VC investment, and heavy equipment used in construction and farming feature a U-shape with respect to the length of ownership prior to sale, while the frequency of trade is hump-shaped for real estate transactions and for sales of VC¬†assets.
